data.title: Pharma companies welcome new year with 583 drug price hikes so far
data.description:
Drugmakers have started 2025, implementing price hikes for 583 products within the first two days of the new year alone, 46brooklyn Research, an Ohio non-profit focused on U.S. drug pricing data, said in a report on Thursday.

Considering the drugs’ wholesale acquisition cost (WAC) or list prices, the firm said that 590 pricing changes, including 583 price hikes and seven decreases, had occurred at the start of the new year.

While nearly a third of WAC increases have occurred on Jan. 1 over the past seven years, this year witnessed more list price increases than any other year, 46brooklyn Research said, citing data from 2011.

According to its report, except for Merck’s (NYSE:MRK) diabetes therapy, Januvia, for which WAC has dropped 42%, all the drugs subject to 2026 Medicare price negotiations have seen list price increases.

They include a 3% WAC price increase implemented for Eli Lilly’s (NYSE:LLY) diabetes therapy Jardiance and a 2% increase introduced for Eliquis, a blood thinner from Bristol Myers (NYSE:BMY) and Pfizer (NYSE:PFE).

Other notable price hikes include a 3% increase for Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) GLP-1 diabetes therapy Ozempic, a 5.9% increase for Gilead’s (GILD) HIV medication Biktarvy, and an 8% hike for Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) (OTCQX:RHHBF) hemophilia therapy Hemlibra.

Meanwhile, Collegium Pharma (COLL) has implemented a 15%-20% WAC price increase for Nucynta, one of the last opioids to remain on the market without generic alternatives. “This likely represents the last big opportunity for the drug manufacturer to capture whatever additional value is gained from list price changes before the product begins to cede ground to generic competitors,” 46brooklyn Research wrote.

However, its update doesn’t indicate price hikes for products manufactured by pharma majors Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), AstraZeneca (AZN), AbbVie (ABBV), and Vertex Pharma (VRTX).

As for price drops, the firm said that pharma companies have sharply cut the prices of several asthma inhalers and central nervous system (CNS) drugs amid regulatory pushback.

For example, Boehringer Ingelheim has dropped the price of its asthma medication Atrovent HFA by 35%. In 2023, the German drugmaker, along with its rivals, such as AbbVie (ABBV), GSK (GSK), and Teva (TEVA), was subject to an FTC warning related to patent violations over asthma inhalers and other products.

data.title: Shell, Chevron start oil production at Gulf of Mexico's Whale project
data.description:
Shell (NYSE:SHEL) said Thursday it began production at its Whale floating facility in the Gulf of Mexico, which is expected to reach peak output of 100K boe/day; Whale is operator and 60% owned by Shell, with the other 40% held by Chevron (CVX).

Shell (SHEL) said Whale, which holds a recoverable resource volume of 480M boe, achieved first oil around seven years after the development was discovered, primarily due to a delay in reaching a final investment decision following the company's cash preservation strategy adopted during the COVID-19 pandemic.

The company expects its operations at Whale will have a greenhouse gas emissions intensity ~30% lower than its nearby Vito floating platform due to the use of more efficient turbines and compression systems.

After selling its onshore Permian Basin business to ConocoPhillips in recent years, Shell's only U.S. production is in the Gulf of Mexico.

Chevron (CVX) said production from the Whale development would bring it closer to reaching 300K net boe/day in the Gulf of Mexico by 2026.

data.title: Peacock president Kelly Campbell steps down
data.description:
• Kelly Campbell, the president of NBC-owned streaming service Peacock, is stepping down, according to multiple media reports from Wednesday.
  • Campbell was previously president of Hulu, where she led the SVOD and live-TV streaming businesses, following a role as its chief marketing officer.
  • Prior to that, she spent 12 years at Google in various leadership roles across the Google Ads and Google Cloud businesses. She began her career as an investment banker at JPMorgan Chase.

data.title: Amentum awarded $447M Air Forces Central Command Global Prepositioned Material Services contract
data.description:
• Amentum (NYSE:AMTM) has been awarded the $447M Air Forces Central Command Global Prepositioned Material Services (GPMS) contract to deliver and modernize prepositioned storage and maintenance solutions.
  • Amentum will leverage proven technologies for smart asset management and a customized data management system.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Elon Musk doubts DOGE can achieve $2T in U.S. budget cuts under Trump
data.description:
Elon Musk, the tech billionaire tapped for a government efficiency panel by President-elect Donald Trump, has expressed skepticism about the feasibility of cutting $2T from the U.S. federal budget, backtracking on his own previously ambitious claims.

The $2T goal was a "best-case outcome," Musk said in an interview broadcast on social media platform X with political strategist Mark Penn, noting "we've got a good shot" at getting half of that.

Reaching that figure would probably require cutting back on programs like Social Security and Medicare, which enjoy stronger voter support and which Trump promised to preserve during his campaign.

In fiscal-year 2024, out of the $6.75T in government expenditures, over $5.3T was allocated to Social Security, health care, defense, veterans' benefits and debit interest payments.

The hardest area to tackle has been popular entitlement programs like Medicare, Medicaid and Social Security, which have been major contributors to the deficit. With no easy solutions there, Trump has tasked Tesla (TSLA) CEO Musk and former Republican presidential candidate Vivek Ramaswamy to head up the newly formed Department of Government Efficiency, or DOGE, to make government operations more efficient.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: iHeartMedia extends podcast partnership with the NFL
data.description:
• iHeartMedia (NASDAQ:IHRT) has extended its partnership with the NFL Podcast Network, the company said in a statement Wednesday.
  • As part of the deal, new original podcasts and NFL Club shows will debut each year, and existing favorites will continue to be available on the NFL Podcast Network, the company said
  • Additionally, the company will identify other existing podcasts to join the NFL Podcast Network during the deal to grow the partnership’s reach and scale.
  • Press Release.

data.title: Equinox jumps following record-breaking Q4, full-year production
data.description:
Equinox Gold (NYSE:EQX) +4.9% in Toronto trading on Thursday after reporting company records for Q4 2024 and full-year gold production, totaling 213,960 oz in Q4 and 621,870 oz for FY 2024.

Full-year production came in within company guidance of 590K-675K oz from its seven operating gold mines in Canada, the U.S., Mexico and Brazil.

Equinox (EQX) CEO Greg Smith attributed the company’s growth primarily to the Greenstone mine in Ontario, having consolidated 100% ownership, producing more than 111,700 oz of gold in the mine's first, partial year of operations.

At full production, Greenstone will be one of Canada’s largest and highest-grade open-pit gold mines, with output estimated at 390K oz/year on average for the first five years and 330K oz/year over an initial 15-year mine life.

Also, the Mesquite mine in California produced 71,980 oz, topping full-year guidance of 55K-65K oz, and output at Castle Mountain totaled 20,510 oz, exceeding guidance of 15K oz.

data.title: U.S. auto industry bracing for new trade and regulatory landscape
data.description:
The U.S. auto industry is bracing for a shifting landscape, marked by the economic policies and global trade strategies of a Trump White House, as it sets itself apart from the outgoing administration.

J.P. Morgan auto analysts met with executives from the top U.S. carmakers to understand what strategies are in place, and how the industry will respond to these challenges, especially how it pertains to the electric vehicle industry.

The majority of industry executives expect the Trump administration to substantially curtail or eliminate the portion of the Inflation Reduction Act which extends tax credits to EV buyers. J.P. Morgan’s auto team expects this will be initially net negative for General Motors (GM), and to a lesser extent Ford (NYSE:F) over the 2025 and potentially 2026 time frame as EV subsidies are removed, before flipping to a net positive as ICE restrictions are eased. The potential end of subsidies is also creating a “pre-buy” environment that could lead to strong sales short-term. The changes are also seen as highly negative for Tesla (TSLA), threatening ~40% of profits and casting significant doubt on the sharp post-election rally in TSLA shares.

The auto industry is also bracing for higher tariffs and warn that the 25% tariff proposed on Mexican and Canadian imports would be “enormously disruptive” to the domestic auto industry. J. P. Morgan expects this will have a much greater detrimental impact to Aptiv (APTV) among suppliers, and GM (GM) more than Ford (NYSE:F).

The confluence of likely regulatory changes will make the business of producing and selling battery electric vehicles “substantially” less profitable, precluding GM (GM) from lessening EV losses by $2B to $4B in 2025, J.P. Morgan says.

GM (GM) is currently implementing various no- and low-cost options to prepare for import tariffs. If steep tariffs do come, management told J.P. Morgan it expects to choose from a range of “coping mechanisms” that include price increases, cost cuts, and lower cost rebalancing of capacity. What GM (GM) is not going to do is spend large amounts of capital in anticipation of threatened tariffs or even after actual implementation, given the potentially short-lived nature of a trade conflict.

Ford (F) faces challenges specific to its EV lineup, namely creating a cost-effective EV from an existing ICE platform. The company said it expects an EV profit inflection in 2026 following the launch of second-generation EV models that had been designed from the beginning as EVs rather than being repurposed from an ICE model.

“Because [EVs] began life as internal combustion engine variants before later being retrofitted as BEVs, there is realistically only so much improvement that can be realized until they are increasingly replaced by second-generation vehicles that have been designed on all-new architectures designed from the beginning to be BEVs and therefore cost and performance optimized for this purpose,” Ford CFO John Lawler told J.P. Morgan, adding that “these vehicles will mostly launch around the end of 2025, and so it is reasonable to assume that the progress toward 2026 Model e margin targets may be back-end loaded.”

J.P. Morgan has an Overweight rating for both Ford (F) and General Motors (GM).

data.title: Delta Air Lines bets a new partnership with DraftKings can pay off
data.description:
Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL) teased some sort of entry into the sports betting world by announcing at CES a new partnership with DraftKings (NASDAQ:DKNG)

"From fantasy sports to online adventures, gaming has become a regular part of the daily lives for millions of our customers, and you shouldn’t have to hit pause just because you’re in the skies," stated CEO Ed Bastian. He said the new partnership with DraftKings (NASDAQ:DKNG) will build on the game portfolio that the airline company already offers via Delta Sync and on seatback screens.

Further details about the partnership were not provided, but there is speculation that SkyMiles earning opportunities for sports betting activities through DraftKings (DKNG) could be part of the arrangement, as well as integration of DraftKings (DKNG) content into Delta's (NYSE:DAL) in-flight entertainment system.

Currently, onboard gambling through an airline's in-flight entertainment system is not legal under U.S. law. The Gambling Devices Act of 1962 (Johnson Act) makes gambling illegal on U.S. commercial aircraft, while the Gorton Amendment prohibits gambling on both U.S.-registered aircraft and any plane flying to or from the U.S. However, airlines can offer gambling on flights that are exclusively outside the U.S. and do not originate from, terminate in, or fly over United States territory.

data.title: Airbus’s yearly deliveries rise 4% to 766 commercial planes
data.description:
Airbus’s (OTCPK:EADSY) (OTCPK:EADSF) deliveries of commercial planes rose 4.2% from a year earlier to 766 in 2024, the highest in five years. The delivery total likely affirms that the European aviation company maintained its spot as the world’s biggest maker of jetliners, as U.S. rival Boeing (NYSE:BA) struggles to overcome safety issues and supply-chain constraints.

Airbus (OTCPK:EADSY) (OTCPK:EADSF) had set a yearly target of about 770 jet deliveries amid its own efforts to overcome shortages in labor and parts.

The company received 878 gross orders and a net total of 826 after cancellations, down 61% from a record 2023.

Boeing (NYSE:BA) had 370 net orders after cancellations by the end of November. The company is due to report deliveries on Tuesday.

The Federal Aviation Administration last year capped Boeing’s (BA) output of its best-selling 737 Max because of safety concerns. A 737 Max 9 model flown by Alaska Airlines lost a door panel shortly after takeoff, forcing an emergency landing in Portland, Oregon. Boeing's (BA) output also was hampered by a seven-week strike by machinists.
